allergens: none identified (gluten-free batter; check oil and accompaniments)

serving.description: 1 thosai with masala filling and accompanying chutneys/sambar
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of masala thosai in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising one large fermented rice-lentil crepe filled with spiced potato masala and served with sambar and coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Masala Thosai — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Masala Thosai (cr_id F05010005), confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained at their reported values but confidence is noted as 0.70. Saturated fat value cross-checked as plausible for a lightly oiled fermented crepe with potato filling. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — Dosa/Thosai analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for masala thosai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: plain dosa/thosai made from fermented rice-urad dal batter, which has a reported GI of approximately 55 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2021 international GI tables). The potato masala filling may modestly raise the effective GI; however, the fermentation process and lentil content moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 x 23.3 x 3.5) / 100 = 44.8, rounded to 45.0 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 346 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,211 mg sodium, approaching 50-60% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in the batter and sambar, limit ghee or butter toppings to reduce saturated fat, and pair masala thosai with potassium-rich accompaniments such as tomato-based sambar to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 23.3 g/100 g with a low GI of 55; a 350 g serving provides approximately 81.6 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 45, which is high for a single meal item.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200-250 g, request a smaller potato filling or substitute with mixed vegetables to lower the glycaemic load, avoid added sugar in chutneys, and pair with protein-rich sambar to moderate the post-meal glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 346 mg/100 g and saturated fat at 1.33 g/100 g; a 350 g serving contributes approximately 1,211 mg sodium and 4.7 g saturated fat, both of which are relevant to cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose masala thosai prepared with minimal oil and no ghee, request low-salt sambar, and limit portion size to 200-250 g to keep sodium and saturated fat within heart-healthy daily targets; the dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide modest cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, cumin) and plant protein from lentils; however, nightshade ingredients (potato, green chilli, tomato) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the curcumin and gingerol content of the spice blend, but should monitor personal tolerance to nightshade vegetables (potato, chilli, tomato) and limit ghee to keep saturated fat low.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala thosai (also spelled masala dosai or masala dosa) is a South Indian fermented rice-and-lentil crepe filled with a spiced potato masala mixture. In Singapore, it is a staple of mamak (Indian-Muslim) and South Indian restaurant menus, typically served with sambar (a lentil-based vegetable soup) and coconut chutney. The batter is made from fermented ground rice and urad dal (black lentil), which gives the thosai its characteristic slight sourness, crisp golden exterior, and soft interior. The masala filling consists of mashed or diced potato cooked with long beans, carrot, green peas, green chilli, onion, mustard seeds, turmeric, and curry leaves. Per 100 g, masala thosai provides 161 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 5.9 g fat (1.33 g saturated), 23.3 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 346 mg sodium. The fermentation process lowers the glycaemic index to approximately 55 (low GI per University of Sydney classification), making it more glycaemically moderate than plain white rice. A standard Singapore serving of approximately 350 g delivers a glycaemic load of around 45, which is significant for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free by traditional preparation. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (346 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients, while the low GI and high per-serving carbohydrate load require portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides polyphenols with evidence-based benefits for inflammatory conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95) for the exact dish (Masala Thosai, cr_id F05010005). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C) retained from input at confidence 0.70 as plausible for this dish type. GI estimated from closest analogous approved-source entry (fermented dosa, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Multilingual names, ontology, and health context are well-supported for this canonical Singapore mamak dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for masala thosai not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry; an estimate of approximately 220 mg/100 g was referenced in the input health_suitability text but this is not from an approved source, so -1 is used. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry and no approved secondary source data available for this exact dish. 5. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0: masala thosai is a vegetarian dish with no animal-derived fat in the batter or filling, making 0.0 mg cholesterol plausible and consistent with the input HPB record; confirmed zero for this vegetarian preparation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100 g (trace level from cooking oil); this is a plausible trace value for a pan-fried dish. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fermented dosa/thosai entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for masala thosai was found. GI 55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database thresholds (low: 55 or below). 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 350 g standard serving: (55 x 23.3 x 3.5) / 100 = 44.8, rounded to 45.0. 9. Two portions in the input had duplicate labels with different weights (300 g and 350 g); the 350 g value was selected as the standard serving based on majority of input portion entries. 10. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are closest_parent matches for dosa/thosai; no exact masala thosai code exists in either system as of 2024. 11. Aliases consistency check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. The Tamil alias 'மசாலா டோசா' and Hindi alias 'मसाला डोसा' confirmed present in flat aliases array.
